g.data-narration: Welcome to Cache Miss Allocation Policy. We explore how systems handle data when cache misses occur.
Cache memory sits between CPU and main memory, storing frequently accessed data for faster access.
A cache hit occurs when data is found in cache. A cache miss requires fetching from slower main memory.
Three types of cache misses: compulsory cold misses on first access, capacity misses when cache is full, and conflict misses from mapping collisions.
g.text: Cache too small for working set
Multiple blocks map to same set

g.data-narration: Fetch-on-Miss allocates cache space immediately upon a miss, loading the block before CPU access completes.
g.text: When a cache miss occurs, the system fetches the block from main memory
and loads it INTO the cache BEFORE delivering data to the CPU.

g.data-narration: Fetch-on-Miss improves temporal locality for subsequent accesses but adds latency on first access and may waste cache space.
g.text: May waste cache for one-time data

g.data-narration: Write-Allocate fetches the block into cache on a write miss, then performs the write operation on cached data.
g.text: Specifically for write operations. When a write miss occurs:
1. Fetch block from main memory 2. Allocate in cache 3. Modify data in cache
Writes to both cache and memory
Writes to cache, updates memory later

g.data-narration: No-Allocate writes data directly to main memory, bypassing the cache entirely on write misses.
g.text: Does NOT bring block into cache on a write miss:
Write goes directly to main memory, bypassing cache.

g.data-narration: Compare policies: fetch-on-miss improves subsequent access but has higher first-access latency. No-allocate prevents cache pollution but misses write locality.
Placement policies determine where blocks can be placed: direct-mapped allows one location, fully associative allows any location, set-associative is a hybrid.
Eviction policies select which block to replace: LRU tracks recency, FIFO evicts oldest, Random is simple but unpredictable.
g.text: When cache is full, which block to evict?

g.data-narration: Example: Direct-mapped cache with 4 lines. Block 5 maps to line 1. First access is a miss, subsequent accesses are hits.
g.text: Direct-mapped cache (4 lines), Access: Read Block 5 → Read Block 5 → Read Block 5

g.data-narration: Comparing write-allocate and no-allocate: write-allocate performs better when there is temporal locality in write operations.
g.text: 2-way set-associative cache, 1 set. Operations: Write A, Write B, Write A, Read A
A→MM, B→MM, A→MM, Read A→MM: 5 MM accesses

g.data-narration: Key formulas: AMAT equals hit time plus miss rate multiplied by miss penalty. Cache line mapping uses modulo arithmetic.
In summary, allocation policy determines how miss data is handled. Choose fetch-on-miss for locality, no-allocate for streaming writes.
g.text: 1. Allocation on miss brings data into cache
5. Choose policy based on access patterns
